title: Find peak element (simple version)
description: Find a peak element in an array where it is greater than its neighbors

examples.explanation: Step 1: Array length 4 > 1, proceed. Step 2: Iterate i=1: arr[1]=2, compare to arr[0]=1 and arr[2]=3 → 2 > 1 but 2 < 3, not peak. Step 3: i=2: arr[2]=3, compare to arr[1]=2 and arr[3]=1 → 3 > 2 and 3 > 1, peak found. Return index 2.
Step 1: Length 7 > 1. Step 2: i=1: arr[1]=2 > arr[0]=1 and arr[2]=1 → peak at index 1. Return 1. (Note: index 5 is also a peak but algorithm returns first found.)

approaches.name: Approach 1: Linear Scan O(n)
approaches.summary: Iterate through the array and check each element to see if it is greater than its neighbors. Return the first peak found, as the problem only requires one peak.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Peak Element Using Linear Scan
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find any peak element in an array using linear iteration.
ladders.explanation: The linear scan approach solves the peak finding problem by examining each element sequentially to check if it's greater than its neighbors. This works because the problem only requires finding ANY peak, not necessarily the global maximum. The algorithm starts by handling edge cases: arrays with 1 element (always a peak) and arrays with 2 elements (return the larger one). For arrays with 3+ elements, it iterates from index 1 to n-2, checking if arr[i] > arr[i-1] and arr[i] > arr[i+1]. If found, return i. If no internal peak exists, check the first element against the second and last element against second-to-last. The intuition is that in any array, there must be at least one peak because if you traverse from left to right, either you'll find an element larger than its right neighbor (making it a peak if also larger than left) or the last element will be a peak. Time complexity is O(n) as we potentially examine all elements, and space is O(1) using only a few variables. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete peak-checking logic within the full problem context. Common mistakes include: not handling edge cases (empty arrays, single element), incorrect boundary checks (accessing arr[-1] or arr[n]), and forgetting to check array ends separately. Real code pattern: def find_peak(arr): if len(arr) == 1: return 0; for i in range(1, len(arr)-1): if arr[i] > arr[i-1] and arr[i] > arr[i+1]: return i; if arr[0] > arr[1]: return 0; return len(arr)-1.

examples.trace: Step 1: Array length 4 > 1, proceed. Step 2: Iterate i=1: arr[1]=2, compare to arr[0]=1 and arr[2]=3 → 2 > 1 but 2 < 3, not peak. Step 3: i=2: arr[2]=3, compare to arr[1]=2 and arr[3]=1 → 3 > 2 and 3 > 1, peak found. Return index 2.
Step 1: Length 7 > 1. Step 2: i=1: arr[1]=2 > arr[0]=1 and arr[2]=1 → peak at index 1. Return 1. (Note: index 5 is also a peak but algorithm returns first found.)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Check If Element Is Peak
ladders.desc: Determine if a given array element is greater than its neighbors.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine checks whether an element at a specific index is a peak by comparing it with its immediate neighbors. The function takes an array and an index as input and returns True if the element is greater than both neighbors, False otherwise. For edge indices (first or last element), it only compares with the single existing neighbor. The intuition is that a peak is defined locally: an element is a peak if it's not smaller than its adjacent elements. This is the core operation repeated in the linear scan. Step-by-step: 1. Get array length n. 2. If index is 0: return True if arr[0] > arr[1] (if n>1), else True (if n=1). 3. If index is n-1: return True if arr[n-1] > arr[n-2] (if n>1), else True. 4. For internal indices: return True if arr[i] > arr[i-1] and arr[i] > arr[i+1]. Time O(1), space O(1). This feeds into L0 by being called for each element during iteration. Common mistakes: not handling single-element arrays, comparing with wrong indices, or not considering edge cases. Real code: def is_peak(arr, i): n=len(arr); if n==1: return True; if i==0: return arr[0]>arr[1]; if i==n-1: return arr[n-1]>arr[n-2]; return arr[i]>arr[i-1] and arr[i]>arr[i+1].

examples.trace: Step 1: n=3, i=1 is internal. Step 2: Compare arr[1]=3 with arr[0]=1 and arr[2]=2. Step 3: 3>1 and 3>2 → True.
Step 1: n=4, i=0 is first element. Step 2: Compare arr[0]=5 with arr[1]=4. Step 3: 5>4 → True.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Sequential Peak Detection
ladders.desc: Iterate through array indices to find first element satisfying peak condition.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves scanning the array from left to right and returning the index of the first element that is a peak. The algorithm leverages the fact that we only need one peak, so we can stop at the first occurrence. The intuition: as we move through the array, we're looking for a 'hill'—an element that is higher than both sides. If the array is strictly increasing, the last element is a peak; if strictly decreasing, the first is a peak; otherwise, there's an internal peak where the trend changes. Steps: 1. Handle arrays of size 1 (return 0). 2. Check if first element is peak (compare to second). 3. Loop i from 1 to n-2: if arr[i] > arr[i-1] and arr[i] > arr[i+1], return i. 4. If loop completes, check last element (compare to second-last). Time O(n) worst-case, O(1) best-case if peak early; space O(1). This is the central technique of L0, using L1's check. Common mistakes: infinite loops, off-by-one errors in loop range, not checking array ends. Real code: def find_first_peak(arr): n=len(arr); if n==1: return 0; if arr[0]>arr[1]: return 0; for i in range(1,n-1): if arr[i]>arr[i-1] and arr[i]>arr[i+1]: return i; return n-1.

examples.trace: Step 1: n=5>1. Step 2: arr[0]=1 not > arr[1]=2. Step 3: i=1: arr[1]=2 not > arr[2]=3. i=2: arr[2]=3 > arr[1]=2 and > arr[3]=2 → return 2.
Step 1: n=4>1. Step 2: arr[0]=4 > arr[1]=3 → return 0 immediately.

ladders.title: Building Block — Compare Element with Neighbors
ladders.desc: Given an array and index, retrieve and compare values with adjacent elements.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves accessing an array element and its neighbors, then performing comparisons to determine if the element is larger. It's fundamental to peak checking. The intuition: to decide if an element is a peak, we need to examine its local context—the elements immediately before and after. Steps: 1. Access arr[i]. 2. If i>0, access arr[i-1]; else, treat as no left neighbor. 3. If i< n-1, access arr[i+1]; else, treat as no right neighbor. 4. Compare values: for internal i, check arr[i] > arr[i-1] and arr[i] > arr[i+1]; for edges, check against single neighbor. Time O(1), space O(1). This feeds into L2 by providing the comparison result for each index. Common mistakes: index out of bounds, incorrect comparison operators, not handling edges. Real code: def compare_with_neighbors(arr,i): left=arr[i-1] if i>0 else None; right=arr[i+1] if i<len(arr)-1 else None; if left is None: return arr[i]>right; if right is None: return arr[i]>left; return arr[i]>left and arr[i]>right.

examples.trace: Step 1: Access arr[1]=10. Step 2: i=1>0 so left=arr[0]=5. Step 3: i=1<2 so right=arr[2]=8. Step 4: Compare: 10>5 and 10>8 → True.
Step 1: Access arr[1]=1. Step 2: i=1>0 so left=arr[0]=3. Step 3: i=1=len-1 so right=None. Step 4: Compare: 1>3? False.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Indexing and Access
ladders.desc: Retrieve element value from array using index.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves accessing an element in an array given its index. In programming, arrays are zero-indexed: the first element is at index 0, second at index 1, etc. The intuition: arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations, and indexing allows direct access in O(1) time. Steps: 1. Have array arr and integer index i. 2. Check if i is within valid range [0, len(arr)-1]. 3. Use syntax arr[i] to get value. Time O(1), space O(1). This is used in L3 to get element and neighbor values. Common mistakes: using negative indices incorrectly, accessing out of bounds, misunderstanding zero-indexing. Real code: value = arr[i] if 0 <= i < len(arr) else error.

examples.trace: Step 1: Array length=3, valid indices 0,1,2. Step 2: i=1 is valid. Step 3: Access arr[1] → value 20.
Step 1: Length=1, valid index 0. Step 2: i=0 valid. Step 3: Access arr[0] → 5.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What Is a Peak?
ladders.desc: Understand the definition of a peak element in a sequence.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept defines a peak element: in an array, an element is a peak if it is greater than or equal to its immediate neighbors. For this problem, we use strict greater-than for simplicity. Key points: 1. For internal elements (not first or last), compare with both left and right neighbors. 2. For first element, only compare with right neighbor. 3. For last element, only compare with left neighbor. 4. In arrays of size 1, the single element is trivially a peak. Intuition: think of the array as a landscape—peaks are local maxima, valleys are local minima. This concept is prerequisite for all higher levels. Time/space not applicable as it's a definition. Connects to L4 by needing to access elements to compare. Common mistakes: confusing peak with global maximum, not handling edge cases, using wrong comparison. Real code: conceptually, a peak satisfies: if i==0: arr[i]>=arr[i+1]; if i==n-1: arr[i]>=arr[i-1]; else: arr[i]>=arr[i-1] and arr[i]>=arr[i+1].

examples.trace: Step 1: Element at index 0: value 2, compare to right neighbor 4 → 2<4, not peak. Step 2: Index 1: value 4, compare to left 2 and right 3 → 4>2 and 4>3, peak. Step 3: Index 2: value 3, compare to left 4 → 3<4, not peak.
Step 1: Only one element, no neighbors → it is a peak by definition.

approaches.name: Approach 2: Binary Search O(log n)
approaches.summary: Use binary search on the array by comparing the middle element with its neighbors to determine which half must contain a peak. This works because the array is not necessarily sorted but has a peak property.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Binary Search Peak Finder
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to find any peak element in an array using binary search.
ladders.explanation: This solution leverages binary search on an unsorted array by exploiting the peak property. The intuition is that if you examine the middle element and its neighbor, you can guarantee that at least one half contains a peak. Specifically, if nums[mid] < nums[mid+1], then the right half must contain a peak because either it continues increasing to a peak or eventually decreases. Conversely, if nums[mid] < nums[mid-1], the left half must contain a peak. This works because the array boundaries are considered -∞, ensuring a peak exists. The algorithm initializes left=0, right=n-1. While left < right, compute mid = (left+right)//2. If nums[mid] < nums[mid+1], set left = mid+1 (peak in right half). Else, set right = mid (peak in left half). When left == right, it's a peak. Time: O(log n) as we halve search space each iteration. Space: O(1) using pointers. Connection to L1: This uses the binary search subroutine to narrow down the peak. Common mistakes: Using left <= right can cause infinite loops; handle edge cases with single-element arrays. Real code pattern: def findPeakElement(nums): left, right = 0, len(nums)-1; while left < right: mid = (left+right)//2; if nums[mid] < nums[mid+1]: left = mid+1; else: right = mid; return left.

examples.trace: Step 1: left=0, right=3, mid=1, nums[1]=2, nums[2]=3, 2<3 → left=2. Step 2: left=2, right=3, mid=2, nums[2]=3, nums[3]=1, 3>1 → right=2. Step 3: left=2, right=2 → return 2.

Step 1: left=0, right=6, mid=3, nums[3]=3, nums[4]=5, 3<5 → left=4. Step 2: left=4, right=6, mid=5, nums[5]=6, nums[6]=4, 6>4 → right=5. Step 3: left=4, right=5, mid=4, nums[4]=5, nums[5]=6, 5<6 → left=5. Step 4: left=5, right=5 → return 5.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Binary Search on Peak Property
ladders.desc: Implement binary search that compares middle element with neighbor to decide search direction.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core of the peak-finding algorithm. The intuition: In any array with -∞ boundaries, if you pick a middle index, comparing nums[mid] with nums[mid+1] tells you which half must contain a peak. If nums[mid] < nums[mid+1], the right half has higher values, so a peak exists there (values must eventually decrease or hit boundary). If nums[mid] >= nums[mid+1], the left half (including mid) might have a peak. We use left < right to ensure convergence. Pseudocode: left=0, right=n-1; while left < right: mid = (left+right)//2; if nums[mid] < nums[mid+1]: left = mid+1; else: right = mid; return left. Time: O(log n), space: O(1). Connection to L0: This subroutine directly solves the full problem. Common mistakes: Using <= in loop condition; not handling single-element arrays. Real code pattern: def binarySearchPeak(nums): left, right = 0, len(nums)-1; while left < right: mid = (left+right)//2; if nums[mid] < nums[mid+1]: left = mid+1; else: right = mid; return left.

examples.trace: Step 1: left=0, right=3, mid=1, nums[1]=2, nums[2]=3, 2<3 → left=2. Step 2: left=2, right=3, mid=2, nums[2]=3, nums[3]=1, 3>1 → right=2. Step 3: left=2, right=2 → return 2.
Step 1: left=0, right=2, mid=1, nums[1]=2, nums[2]=1, 2>1 → right=1. Step 2: left=0, right=1, mid=0, nums[0]=3, nums[1]=2, 3>2 → right=0. Step 3: left=0, right=0 → return 0.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Peak Guarantee in Halves
ladders.desc: Understand why comparing middle element with neighbor guarantees a peak in one half.
ladders.explanation: The central insight is that for any index i in an array with -∞ boundaries, if nums[i] < nums[i+1], then there must be a peak in the subarray from i+1 to end. Why? Consider walking right from i+1: values start at nums[i+1] > nums[i]. If they keep increasing, the last element is a peak (since boundary is -∞). If they decrease at some point, that point is a peak. Similarly, if nums[i] >= nums[i+1], the left half (0 to i) must contain a peak. This creates a monotonic property allowing binary search. Pseudocode to test: given nums, left, right, mid, if nums[mid] < nums[mid+1], peak in [mid+1, right]; else peak in [left, mid]. Time: O(1) per comparison, space: O(1). Connection to L1: This logic drives the binary search decision. Common mistakes: Assuming array is sorted; not considering boundary conditions. Real code pattern: def peakInHalf(nums, left, right): mid = (left+right)//2; if nums[mid] < nums[mid+1]: return 'right'; else: return 'left'.

examples.trace: nums[1]=2, nums[2]=3. Since 2<3, the right half [2,3,1] must contain a peak. Indeed, index 2 is a peak.
nums[1]=2, nums[2]=1. Since 2>1, the left half [3,2] must contain a peak. Indeed, index 0 is a peak.

ladders.title: Building Block — Binary Search on Indices
ladders.desc: Implement binary search to find a target index in a sorted array.
ladders.explanation: Binary search efficiently finds an element in a sorted array by repeatedly dividing the search interval. Intuition: If the array is sorted, comparing the middle element with the target tells you which half to search. Pseudocode: left=0, right=n-1; while left <= right: mid = (left+right)//2; if nums[mid] == target: return mid; elif nums[mid] < target: left = mid+1; else: right = mid-1; return -1. Time: O(log n), space: O(1). Connection to L2: This is the algorithmic template adapted for peak finding by changing the comparison logic. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in loop condition; incorrect mid calculation. Real code pattern: def binarySearch(nums, target): left, right = 0, len(nums)-1; while left <= right: mid = (left+right)//2; if nums[mid] == target: return mid; elif nums[mid] < target: left = mid+1; else: right = mid-1; return -1.

examples.trace: Step 1: left=0, right=4, mid=2, nums[2]=3 == target → return 2.
Step 1: left=0, right=3, mid=1, nums[1]=20 < 25 → left=2. Step 2: left=2, right=3, mid=2, nums[2]=30 > 25 → right=1. Step 3: left=2, right=1 → exit loop, return -1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compare Array Elements with Neighbors
ladders.desc: Check if an element is greater than its immediate neighbors.
ladders.explanation: This operation checks if an array element at index i is a peak: nums[i] > nums[i-1] and nums[i] > nums[i+1], with special handling for boundaries where missing neighbors are considered -∞. Intuition: A peak is a local maximum. For interior indices, compare with both neighbors; for edges, compare with the single neighbor. Pseudocode: def isPeak(nums, i): if i == 0: return nums[i] > nums[i+1]; elif i == len(nums)-1: return nums[i] > nums[i-1]; else: return nums[i] > nums[i-1] and nums[i] > nums[i+1]. Time: O(1), space: O(1). Connection to L3: This comparison is used in binary search to decide direction. Common mistakes: Not handling edge cases; using >= instead of >. Real code pattern: def isPeak(nums, i): n = len(nums); left = nums[i-1] if i>0 else float('-inf'); right = nums[i+1] if i<n-1 else float('-inf'); return nums[i] > left and nums[i] > right.

examples.trace: nums[2]=3, left neighbor nums[1]=2, right neighbor nums[3]=1. 3>2 and 3>1 → True.
nums[1]=2, left neighbor nums[0]=1, right neighbor nums[2]=3. 2>1 but 2<3 → False.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Comparison
ladders.desc: Access array elements by index and compare numerical values.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept involves accessing elements in an array using indices and comparing their values. An array is a collection of elements stored at contiguous indices starting from 0. To access an element, use array[index]. Comparison operators (>, <, ==) compare numerical values. Intuition: Arrays allow random access; indices must be within bounds. Pseudocode: element = arr[i]; compare: if arr[i] > arr[j]: ... Time: O(1) per access/comparison, space: O(1). Connection to L4: This is used to check neighbors for peaks. Common mistakes: Index out of bounds; confusing indices with values. Real code pattern: arr = [10, 20, 30]; val = arr[1]; # val=20; if arr[0] < arr[1]: print('increasing').

examples.input: Array: [5,10,15], access index 1, compare with index 0
examples.trace: arr[1] = 10, arr[0] = 5. Compare: 10 > 5 is True.

examples.input: Array: [3,1,4], access index 2, compare with index 1
examples.trace: arr[2] = 4, arr[1] = 1. Compare: 4 > 1 is True.
